sentences.text: Well, they were making quite often segregation.
People who didn't have the Kennkarte-- this is the card with the stamp that they work somewhere-- they deport them.
We didn't know then what we know now.
We thought they go to other camp.
But we've never seen them again.
Some very young children and not strong-looking physically, unable to work-- they've been segregating them and take away.
Didn't have a clue then, didn't have a clue.
We--
About '42, end of '42.
The, the Jewish police came and took them to certain Platz, place.
And then they took them out.
We never see-- I didn't-- we didn't, didn't have contact with them.
See, they had-- there was a big place, and Platz [?
Gold, ?] they called in Cracow.
And there was also the polish chemist on the corner, there, who watch all these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] segregations.
He help a lot Jews, this-- Pankevitch was his name in Cracow.
We play quite often there.
And the commandant of Jewish police, that Spira, was coming there, some or other important people, drinking vodka from the files, you know, chemists-- they got in chemist, from those files drink.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we had to-- not allowed to go after 10 o'clock.
So we had to go under guard back home and held a-- everything very close.
What was told--
They have been told that they are going to other camp.
They told them to take bags.
But, but as soon as they leave, they took away the bags.
You've seen on television-- on film how they have been doing this.
They took the whole possessions and over to oven.
We can't resist with guns standing, police with dogs barking.
I didn't have a clue.
As I said, the surprise on my wedding night, I was taken to concentration camp.
And that was-- as I said before, the, the, the camp was made on the old Jewish cemetery.
They pulled out the grave tombstones and mutilated the, the earth with the big machines digging out.
And a lot of skeletons-- and that digger man with a hammer was knocking out gold teeth from dead people and collecting the gold.
And from the tombstone-- tombstones' marble, they make paving to, to-- the camp commandant was walking on this.
My-- no, my, my brother of my father, he was a gabbai, quite a religious Jew.
He married us.
He was killed in, in Plaszow.
And his wife was killed in Plaszow.
There was-- he was a older brother of my father.
Did I--
No, I told you.
I was taken on the night of the wedding.
My wife came after eight weeks to the same camp.
And of course, we didn't live together.
It was separate barracks for women, separate for men.
And sometime when the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German soldier-- or I'll call it kapo-- came or, or, or German woman went-- caught some woman in men's barrack or vice versa, got big hiding.
Where?
In, in, in barracks, in some barracks.
It was-- were three tiers I called [NON-ENGLISH], beds, you know, one on top, two, three.
A lot of-- you see, you're not allowed to go at night to toilet.
And some people make themselves such a tin to relieve themself.
Sometimes such a tin was spilled from third down.
A lot of thing happened.
See, and my wife was working in some office, segregating some papers for a short time.
And she light a cigarette, which wasn't allowed.
And suddenly, Goeth came in where she was smoking cigarette, took out the gun, just about to shoot her.
And one German has said, Herr Kommandant, this is wife of the musician Rosner.
He put the gun back.
One second if she wouldn't be my wife, she wouldn't-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
You didn't need to do much.
Just if you walk too slow, you get shot.
You stop for a second carrying bricks-- woman stop for a second to catch breath.
If he notice from balcony, he shoot you.
And it was unpredictable.
Yes.
Quite a lot of people they have been bringing there on one spot, little, little-- not mountain, little higher place-- and with big hole.
And they've been throwing there and put petrol and burning them.
And once they caught cousin of my wife, young boy of 16, while he was working in one of the barracks.
He heard somewhere this tune, 'Katyusha,' a Russian tune, [SINGING] Da, da, da, da, da, da, ra, da, da, da, da.
He was whistling that tune.
And one German denounced him that he often sang Russian national tunes.
That was enough they called 20,000 people on the Appellplatz, on the big Platz.
And they hanged him, this boy.
And the rope broke down.
He start to kiss boots of the commandant, say, Herr Kommandant, I am young.
I want to live-- took out the gun, shoot him.
It's unbelievable.
Once they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one run away from concentration camp.
They caught him.

And he had to parade in front of the 20-- over 20,000 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] inmates with a sign, 'I am back.' And we had to play 'J'attendrai,' the tune-- into German, '[GERMAN],' 'Come back,' that tune.
We had to play that tune while he was parading.
Then they started to one day make segregation of children, start to take all children from parents, from mothers.
That cry is unimaginable, how they put the children-- these mothers crying.
And this is-- on that occasion, my nephew, which was a very young boy-- my two nephews, they run into toilet.
They call latrine-- it's a big room with board with holes.
And they jump into the exhumes.
And they saved their lives through this.
And they took away [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] those children.

And sadistically, they played through the speakers-- loudspeakers, they played the tune, '[SPEAKING GERMAN],' 'Mommy, Buy Me a Little Horsey.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Background-- they played that tune.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes, this is unforgettable.

And when the Russian were coming closer-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't mention before that Oskar Schindler had the factory Emalia Enamel Works on the outskirt of Cracow but not in concentration camp.
And there was working 700 people going under guard every day to work and coming back at night.
But after short time, he used his influence, his bribing, he fix that he was allowed to build barracks in his factory.
So the people didn't have to come back to Plaszow.
That was already like win-- [? tattersall. ?] And no German was-- soldier was allowed to go into factory.
It was unbelievable.
So he had 700 people working there.
And when the Russian were coming closer, he got order to liquidate the factory and to send us-- send the people to Auschwitz.
Instead, he went to Brunnlitz, Brnenec-- this is in Czechoslovakia.
He, he bought old, unused knitting factory-- it was before.
He said that he is going to make a munition factory in there.
And for this, he need 1,300-- I beg your pardon.
I make a mistake. 1,100-- no, it must have been 1,300-- 1,100 people he need for this.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was only 700 in the barrack-- 1,200 was.
I'm forgetting.
He had 700, so another 500.
So they started to make lists.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is how the list started to be.
We've been one of the first on the list because Schindler's-- Schindler like us.
He like our music.
And I took 300 men and 200 women.
Who made the list?
The, the-- on, on, on the instruction, probably, from Amon Goeth or Schindler.
They took some professional men.
But it wasn't many.
But there was a lot of bribing there.
But we've been lucky that we went-- and my brother, with his wife, and my sister, Regina Horovitz, with her husband.
And they had a little boy-- my brother had a little boy, Alex.
And my sister had a little boy, Richard Horovitz.
And I went on the list also.
As you've seen on, on, on the film, how he show-- tried to convince Germans that he got very sensitive shells for guns and that a little hand can go into to clean it.
Big hand wouldn't go in.
He tried to cover them up.
But anyhow, I tell you later on what's happened.
So I've been on the list.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, without any warning, they took all the men that-- on the train-- good train that we are going to Brunnlitz, to Schindler.
But instead, we landed in Gross Rosen, other concentration camp.
I had my accordion with me, and my brother-- violin Guadagnini, which is worth over $50,000.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I thought they wouldn't take from me this instrument.
Very unusual, very important thing happened in Plaszow before I left.
I found some-- I was smoking before the war-- pipe.
And I found some old pipe and got some money.
On black market, I bought the packet of German pipe tobacco.
When I open it, inside was 100 German marks-- original packet.
I haven't got a clue till today where from.
So I thought I would unscrew the side from accordion.
I hide it.
I won't take accordion from me.
I did it.
But unfortunately, when I came to this Gross Rosen, they took away all our possession, all our clothing, give us the stripe suits.
And after standing in shower-- cold shower, standing naked on the open street till evening, they loaded us on the train and took us luckily to Oskar Schindler.
Oh, just about 24 hours.
We didn't know-- you see, first of all, we went into shower.
We heard already then.
We didn't know what would come from the recess, water or gas.
But luckily, it was water.
We didn't know what to expect.
We're shivering, cold, and hungry.
Anyhow, we came to Oskar Schindler--
Standing we sleep.
We, we sit-- we sit in, in this-- like this stretched leg, one next-- like this, like this, no, no, no one half meter to place-- to put your finger in on, on the floor--
--sitting like this.
Hardly, hardly anything.
So we we came to Schindler, we noticed to our horror that there was no women-- our wives.
So we beg.
Herr Schindler, please do something.
Save our wives.
So he used his influence.
He sent one German to Auschwitz, to Birkenau, where my wife was-- there.
The same thing happened to women.
Instead of Brunnlitz, they sent them to Auschwitz, to Birkenau.
And they-- Helene was there-- a few weeks in there in terrible condition, shivering, no food, no drink water.
She was drinking water from gutters.
She got the typhoid.
She was very sick.
And one day, a German came and called by name these 300 women.
That was unheard in history.
By-- was saying that only way out of Auschwitz is through the chimney.
And he called the 300 women.
And my wife was in there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day, they brought them to Brunnlitz.
My wife was in terrible condition.
At other concentration camp, they would shoot her straight away.
But instead, they put her in the hospital.
And she get back to her hurt slowly.
And also I said to Oskar, Herr Schindler, they took our, our instruments, accordion and violin.
Don't worry.
He sent one German.
And with next day, he came.
On his back, he brought my accordion and my brother, the Guadagnini violin, which he had till now.
And I opened accordion-- unscrewed the side.
There was that 100 mark.
You can't imagine what a fortune that was in there, especially my wife was so sick.
I could buy on black markets some food, something extra.
I must save her life.
Until today, I haven't got a clue.
It was sent from God.
There was-- next to us, was a-- was a camp not so strict with Poles.
And through the wire, a tall-- in Brunnlitz wasn't such a-- so strict as other camps.
You see, this factory, he put a huge machines making a lot of noise.
Left hand didn't know what right hand is doing.
And we learned after the war that he was so shrewd, Schindler, he bought some shells in other factory in case when the inspection come to check what we are doing, to show what we are doing.
I was doing some painting job also there.
Helene was working on the lathes--
--metal, metal lathe.
Left hand didn't know what the right hand is doing.
Not one bullet came out of the factory.
Also easy work.
But one second, my brother wasn't there.
He-- after a short time, week or two, the camps ordered they have to take away the children, my brother's son, Alex, and my brother-in-law, Richard Horovitz.
So they didn't want to let them out, didn't want to let them go alone.
They went with them together.
Because they're not-- they are too young, not allowed.
Some, some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody ducked them in, I don't know.
Anyhow, they went with the children to Auschwitz or to Dachau camp.
And up there, one month, see, my sister-in-law was going in Auschwitz-- was in Auschwitz, she saw through the hole of the crack in, in, in, in the train her son.
So she went out of train under the-- suppose that she's relieving herself and had a few words with him from under the train in Auschwitz.
That was in Auschwitz--
--in Auschwitz.
And he's-- they haven't-- didn't have any contact anymore.
And they survived-- Henry with his son and my brother-in-law, [?
Dolek ?] Horovitz, with his son, Richard.
They survived there.
That was near the end of the war.
All was-- already was, was-- and everything was mishmash, you know.
With-- out of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how to say.
Nobody watched them, you know.
It was, was mixture-- mixed up.
See, how Schindler was cunning, He was a chain-smoker, as I said before.
He couldn't give-- some people for a cigarette, would give a life in wartime.
He couldn't take a cigarette-- give a cigarette.
So he took out his cigarette packet, took out one cigarette, light it, one-- pfff-- one puff and throw it away.
One puff, throw it away.
And they can pick from the floor the cigarettes was throwing.
Oh, yes, yes, yes, quite often, and Mrs.
Schindler, also.
See-- and near the end of the war, a few days before the end, she personally saved near-- over a hundred Jews, Mrs.
Schindler.
There was a train going from camp to camp.
Already was German running away because Russians were coming closer.
And nobody want to accept this train.
And she stopped at wagon with the people.
They had to-- it was deep frozen.
They had to use blowtorches to open the wagon.
When they open, half was dead, frozen.
And in interview, Mrs.
Schindler says, first time in my life, I couldn't recognize which is man, which is a woman-- so disfigured.
And those alive, she dressed, cooked food for them, saved over a hundred people, very last minute in, in Brunnlitz.
Now when the Russian were coming very close, Schindler decided to run away with the help of inmates.
He had a, I think, Cadillac car once belonging to a [?
Jakov Persija, ?] [?
Jakov Persija. ?]
Pardon me?
That, that car, car dealer-- belonged to him.
Schindler bought the car from [?
Jakov Persija ?] during the war.
And he had the car.
So in this car, they decided to run away with help of, of friend of mine.
So I dressed, dressed him in this suit, white and blue, the same striped suit and hat.
Schindler was afraid of Germans.
About three or four of my friends.
They been driving the car.
And he pretend to be also one of them.
He was afraid of Germans.
He was afraid of Russians.
He wasn't sure about the Americans, either.
Some, some-- first shoots, then ask questions.
And although he had a letter from-- with 1,300 signatures from Jews that he saved our life.
He luckily came to American Zone.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And up there, the Joint, a Jewish organization, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bought for Schindler and his wife-- bought a farm in Argentina.
It was already end of the war.
Nobody could listen.
You see, one day, you see, I was-- I was-- all the war I was in good health.
And the last day, my nerves gave up.
They took me to hospital just for one day.
And this is the day when I haven't seen Schindler sayings to all the people, you are free now.
You can do what you want.
You can go where you want.
Say goodbye to them.
And this is when one of the inmate took out a crown, a gold crown.
And my brother-in-law, Joseph [?
Gross, ?] is here, husband of a sister of my wife which died three years ago.
He made the ring, gold ring for Oskar Schindler.
And they smuggled him out.
And next day, we've seen Russian there or come Russians already there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we moved out.
We, we went to some-- after Germans, some apartments, we spent few days.
And then we took a open train, good train, going somewhere.
We didn't know where to go.
We wanted to go to Cracow.
What do you mean by 'good train'?
Good train is for carrying goods, you know, open of-- or cattle train, open, open completely without a-- not with the doors.
Well, what I had.
Therefore we left Brunnlitz.
Schindler had the magazine with some materials, some clothing materials, some sleepers, some pajamas.
He give all-- each of us, he give us a pajama and pair of slippers and three yards of material for suit, which-- my grandson gots the suit still here.
Very good material, it was.
And that we had with us.
And I had the accordion.
And I had the violin of my brother because he was taken away from Brunnlitz.
So why are we going--
My wife, some friends.
And while we're going, in middle of nowhere, train stopped.
Some Russian soldiers started to take away things from us.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought, oh, my accordion will go.
And just one wagon before us, I heard the train whistle, [WHISTLING].
And train moved.
I jumped out.
And my accordion was saved.
And violin was saved.
